title: Turn messy dev notes into a crisp changelog entry
content:
Rewrite the notes below into a clean changelog entry.

Requirements:
- Tone: modern, direct, no fluff.
- Output in Markdown.
- Start with a 1-line summary.
- Then sections: Added, Changed, Fixed, Deprecated (omit any empty sections).
- Each bullet starts with a strong verb and stays under 18 words.
- Keep product-specific names, endpoints, and flags exactly as written.
- If something is unclear, keep it but mark with “(needs confirmation)”.
- Do not invent details.

Messy notes:
[PASTE NOTES HERE]

Optional context (version, date, audience):
[PASTE CONTEXT HERE]

title: Turn a Feature List into a Landing Page Hero + Value Props
content:
I’m going to paste a product feature list. Turn it into concise landing page copy.

Input:
- Product name:
- Target audience:
- One-sentence positioning (if any):
- Feature list (bullets):
- Differentiator vs alternatives:
- Primary CTA (e.g., Start free trial / Book a demo):
- Tone constraints (optional):

Output (keep it tight, no fluff):
1) Hero section
- Headline (max 10 words)
- Subhead (max 22 words)
- 3 supporting bullets (benefit-first, not feature-first)
- Primary CTA button text (2–4 words)
- Secondary CTA button text (2–4 words)

2) Value props
- 4 mini-sections. Each mini-section includes:
  - Title (max 5 words)
  - One sentence explanation (max 18 words)
  - One proof hook (choose one: metric, quote stub, or outcome)

3) “How it works”
- 3 steps, each: Step title (max 4 words) + one sentence (max 18 words)

Rules:
- Write in plain language. Prefer concrete outcomes over buzzwords.
- Avoid: “seamless”, “revolutionary”, “world-class”, “cutting-edge”.
- If the input lacks specifics, make neutral assumptions and keep claims modest.
- Use the audience’s vocabulary; if unclear, default to pragmatic B2B SaaS wording.

Feature list:
[PASTE HERE]

title: Cinematic social media banner for a developer notes series (dark UI aesthetic)
content:
Create a premium social media banner image for a “Dev Notes” series.

Scene & subject:
- A minimalist desk setup in a dim studio: matte-black laptop slightly open, a dark-mode code editor on screen (no readable brand/UI text), a small notebook with subtle “DEV NOTES” embossing, and a graphite pencil.
- Add one secondary tech element for depth (e.g., a compact mechanical keyboard or a slim external SSD), kept understated.

Composition:
- Wide horizontal layout (banner), strong negative space on the right for future overlay text.
- Rule-of-thirds: laptop and notebook on the left, empty dark gradient space on the right.
- Subtle foreground blur element (e.g., out-of-focus mug silhouette) for depth.

Lighting & mood:
- Low-key, cinematic lighting with a cool neutral grade (deep navy/charcoal, cool whites, restrained saturation).
- Soft directional key light from upper left, gentle rim light for separation, controlled reflections on metal edges.
- Background: dark, minimal, slightly textured (no bright walls, no warm wood dominance).

Style & realism:
- Photorealistic, high-end product photography feel; crisp details, soft shadows, shallow depth of field.
- Clean, modern, professional—no playful colors, no clutter.

Camera & rendering cues:
- 35–50mm lens look, f/2.8 shallow DOF, subtle film grain, high dynamic range, vignette kept minimal.
- Output: 16:9 banner, 4K.

Avoid:
- Bright daylight, warm/cozy lifestyle tones, visible logos, readable code/text, messy cables, neon colors.

title: Reply to a customer support email with clear next steps (friendly, concise)
content:
Write a friendly, helpful reply email to the customer message below. Keep it concise but complete.

Goals:
- Acknowledge their issue and any frustration without over-apologizing.
- Restate the problem in plain language to confirm understanding.
- Ask only the minimum clarifying questions needed (max 3), and explain why each helps.
- Provide immediate steps they can try right now (numbered), including where to click/type if relevant.
- If you suggest troubleshooting, include what outcome to look for after each step.
- Offer a clear next step if the issue persists (what to send us, what we’ll do, and an expected response window).
- Avoid blaming the user; avoid jargon; no emojis.

Constraints:
- 120–180 words unless the customer’s message is complex.
- Use a helpful subject line.
- Sign off with: “Thanks,\n[Your Name]”

Customer email:
---
[paste customer email here]
---

Context you can assume (edit as needed):
- Product/service: [what it is]
- Plan/tier: [if relevant]
- Known incident/outage: [yes/no + brief]
- Customer platform: [Web/iOS/Android/Desktop] on [OS + version]
- Account identifier available: [email/order ID/etc]

Now draft the reply.

title: Turn a messy business idea into a one-page execution plan
content:
Take the business idea below and turn it into a clear one-page execution plan I can act on this week. Write in a confident, structured tone and keep it practical (no fluff).

Business idea (paste here):
[...]

Context (optional): target customer, geography, budget range, timeline, constraints
[...]

Output exactly in this format:

1) One-sentence concept
- What it is and who it’s for.

2) Target customer (pick one primary segment)
- Segment:
- Pain points (3 bullets):
- Current alternatives:

3) Value proposition
- Why we win (3 bullets):
- Proof/credibility we can use now:

4) Offer & pricing (simple)
- Core offer:
- What’s included:
- Starting price + rationale:

5) Go-to-market (first 14 days)
- Channel 1:
  - Message angle:
  - Daily actions (5 bullets max):
- Channel 2:
  - Message angle:
  - Daily actions (5 bullets max):

6) MVP scope
- Must-have (5 bullets max):
- Nice-to-have (3 bullets max):
- What we are explicitly NOT doing:

7) Success metrics
- Leading indicators (3):
- Lagging indicators (2):
- Target numbers for week 1:

8) Risks & mitigations
- Top 3 risks:
- Mitigation for each:

9) Next actions (checklist)
- A checklist of 10 concrete tasks ordered by priority.

If anything is unclear, make reasonable assumptions and list them under “Assumptions” at the end (max 6 bullets).

title: Turn a rough UI idea into a crisp, buildable screen spec
content:
I’m designing a new screen and I have a rough idea below. Turn it into a concise UI spec my dev team can build without guesswork.

Input (messy notes):
[Paste your notes here]

Output format:
- Screen goal (1 sentence)
- Primary user actions (3–6 bullets)
- Layout: key regions top-to-bottom (bullets)
- Components list (with brief behavior for each)
- States: empty / loading / error / success (what the user sees + what actions are available)
- Validation + constraints (bullets)
- Copy: exact microcopy for labels, buttons, helper text, and error messages
- Accessibility checklist (5–8 bullets)
- Analytics events (event name + when it fires)

Rules:
- Keep it modern and direct. No fluff.
- Make reasonable assumptions, but list them at the end under “Assumptions”.
- Use consistent naming for components and actions so engineers can reference them.

title: Cinematic dark UI product image prompt generator (copy/paste)
content:
Create 5 image-generation prompts for a premium, dark-themed product interface.

Product/subject: [describe the product, object, or concept]
Use case: [app screen, landing hero, feature illustration, background, thumbnail]
Format: [1:1, 4:5, 16:9, 9:16]

Requirements for every prompt:
- Dark, cinematic, low-key lighting; cool/neutral color grading (deep navy, charcoal, cool gray); restrained saturation
- Subdued background that blends into a dark UI; soft shadows; subtle rim light for separation
- Clean composition with clear focal point; minimal clutter; premium materials (glass, anodized metal, matte plastic, subtle texture)
- Avoid bright daylight, warm cozy lifestyle vibes, white backgrounds, strong sun flares, or overly vibrant colors
- Mention camera framing + lens feel (e.g., 50mm, shallow depth of field) and one lighting setup (e.g., softbox key + dim fill)

Output format:
1) Prompt (one paragraph)
2) Negative prompt (short line)
3) Optional: small variations (2 quick tweaks)

Start now using my inputs and make each of the 5 prompts noticeably different (angle, composition, environment, and props) while keeping the same dark premium aesthetic.

title: Cinematic developer desk with code editor glow (dark UI cover image)
content: A premium, low-key cinematic scene of a modern developer workstation at night: a single ultrawide monitor showing a crisp code editor with dark theme (no readable brand names, no real text—use abstract code-like lines), subtle terminal pane, and a minimal window layout. Desk setup: matte black laptop closed to the side, compact mechanical keyboard, graphite mouse, a small notepad with faint scribbles, and a stainless pen; a single dark ceramic mug. Lighting: cool, directional key light from the monitor glow plus a soft rim light from behind, gentle shadows, controlled highlights, slight haze for depth. Color grade: neutral-to-cool (deep navy, charcoal, cool whites), restrained saturation, high-end product-interface vibe. Composition: wide horizontal cover image, lots of clean negative space on the left for UI text overlay, subject weighted to the right third; shallow depth of field with soft bokeh on background elements; subtle film grain. Camera: 35mm, eye-level, f/2.8. Background: subdued, dark wall with faint texture, no bright windows, no warm sunlight, no cozy lifestyle decor. Ultra-detailed, photorealistic, clean and modern.

title: Turn a Messy Dev Brain Dump Into Today’s 3-Task Plan
content:
I’m going to paste a messy brain dump of dev notes (ideas, bugs, links, half-sentences). Please turn it into a simple plan for today.

Output exactly:
1) Top 3 priorities (each with a one-line “why now”)
2) Next actions (3–7 concrete steps, written as verbs)
3) Quick decisions needed (list the smallest decision that unblocks progress)
4) Parking lot (everything else, grouped by theme)
5) 20-minute starter task (the easiest first move to build momentum)

Rules:
- Keep it short, no long explanations.
- If something is unclear, make a reasonable assumption and mark it as “Assumption: …”.
- Use only the info I provide; don’t invent new requirements.

Here are my notes:
<<<PASTE NOTES HERE>>>

title: Turn messy sources into a rigorous research brief with citations and open questions
content:
I’m compiling a research brief and I want it to be rigorous, not a generic summary.

Topic / question:
- [Paste your research question + what decision this brief will support]

Audience:
- [Who will read it, what they already know, and what they need to decide]

Sources (paste any mix of notes, links, quotes, papers, transcripts):
- [Source dump here]

Constraints:
- Time horizon: [e.g., last 2 years / historical]
- Geography / industry scope: [scope]
- What counts as “evidence” for this question: [RCTs, case studies, benchmarks, expert consensus, etc.]
- Output length: [e.g., 1–2 pages]

Deliver a structured brief with the sections below. If you’re uncertain, say so and explain why.

1) Executive answer (<= 8 bullets)
- Directly answer the research question.
- For each bullet, add: confidence (High/Med/Low) + 1 supporting citation.

2) Definitions and scope
- Define key terms as used in the sources.
- Explicitly state what is out of scope.

3) Evidence table
Create a table with columns:
- Claim
- Supporting evidence (quote or concrete datapoint)
- Source (link/title/author/date)
- Study type / methodology (if applicable)
- Sample size / context (if applicable)
- Limitations / confounders
- Your confidence rating

4) Synthesis (what the evidence collectively implies)
- Group findings into 3–6 themes.
- Note where sources agree, disagree, or talk past each other.
- Identify the strongest and weakest parts of the evidence base.

5) Counterarguments and failure modes
- Steelman the best opposing view.
- List plausible reasons the “obvious” conclusion could be wrong in practice.

6) Practical implications
- Translate findings into implications for: strategy, operations, and risk.
- Include “if X, then Y” guidance and where it breaks.

7) Open questions + research plan
- List the top unanswered questions blocking confidence.
- Propose a short research plan (what to read next, what data to collect, who to interview) prioritized by expected value.

8) Citations
- Provide a clean bibliography with stable URLs when possible.
- Use consistent inline citation markers like (Source 3).

Rules:
- Don’t invent sources or statistics. If something isn’t supported, label it as a hypothesis.
- Prefer primary sources; flag secondary reporting.
- When two sources conflict, present both and explain likely reasons (methods, incentives, timeframe, selection bias).
- Keep language crisp and decision-oriented, not academic.

Before writing, ask up to 5 clarifying questions only if essential; otherwise proceed with reasonable assumptions and list them.

title: Turn messy meeting notes into a 10-minute execution plan (owners, deadlines, risks)
content:
Take the meeting notes below and convert them into a crisp execution plan I can act on today.

Requirements:
- Keep it modern and direct. No pep talk.
- Make reasonable assumptions only when necessary; otherwise flag unknowns.
- If something sounds like a decision, capture it as a decision (even if informal).

Output format:
1) Goal (1 sentence)
2) Decisions made (bullets)
3) Action items (table with: Task | Owner | Due date | Priority P0/P1/P2 | Dependencies | Status)
4) Open questions (bullets, written as questions)
5) Risks & mitigations (table: Risk | Impact | Likelihood | Mitigation | Owner)
6) Next check-in: propose a date/time window and a short agenda (max 5 bullets)

Meeting notes:
[PASTE NOTES HERE]

title: Turn a bug report into a prioritized debugging plan (with hypotheses and checks)
content:
I’m going to paste a bug report and any relevant code/log snippets. Turn it into a practical debugging plan I can follow.

Input:
- Bug report: [paste]
- Expected behavior: [paste]
- Actual behavior: [paste]
- Environment (OS/runtime/version/flags): [paste]
- Repro steps (or “not reliably reproducible”): [paste]
- Relevant code snippet(s): [paste]
- Logs/stack traces: [paste]
- Recent changes (commits/PRs/config): [paste]

Output a structured plan with:
1) Fast triage
   - Clarify the failure mode in 1–2 sentences.
   - List the minimum info still needed (only if truly blocking), and how to collect it.
   - Quick checks to rule out common causes (env mismatch, cache, config, permissions, timeouts).

2) Reproduction strategy
   - Most likely way to reproduce locally (exact commands, flags, sample inputs).
   - If flaky: how to increase signal (repeat strategy, logging, toggles, time control, isolation).

3) Hypotheses (ranked)
   For each hypothesis:
   - Why it fits the symptoms
   - What evidence would confirm/refute it
   - The smallest experiment to test it
   - What to inspect (files/functions/modules) based on the snippet

4) Instrumentation plan
   - Specific log lines/metrics to add (with example messages and where to place them).
   - Any guardrails (rate limiting logs, PII concerns).

5) Root-cause isolation
   - A step-by-step decision tree: “If X, then do Y” until narrowed.
   - Include suggested breakpoints/watch expressions if relevant.

6) Fix approach options
   - 2–3 possible fixes with tradeoffs (risk, scope, performance, backwards-compat).
   - Call out any likely unintended side effects.

7) Verification
   - Exact tests to add/update (unit/integration/e2e), including what to assert.
   - Regression checklist (edge cases, error handling, concurrency, timezones, retries).

Constraints:
- Keep it clear and actionable, no generic advice.
- If you reference code changes, show small illustrative snippets or pseudocode, not a full rewrite.
- Assume I want the fastest path to a confident fix with minimal thrash.

title: Cinematic dark UI productivity scene for a note-taking app hero image
content:
Create a premium, cinematic hero image for a productivity/note-taking app.

Subject & scene:
- A sleek laptop on a matte charcoal desk, showing a dark-mode notes interface with a clean outline: “{PROJECT}”, “Today”, “Next”, and a short checklist (no logos, no recognizable brands).
- Subtle accessories: a black mechanical pencil, a minimal notebook, and a muted ceramic mug in the background (keep them secondary).

Composition:
- 3/4 angle from slightly above, tight framing with negative space on the right for UI text overlay.
- Shallow depth of field; the screen content is readable but not tack-sharp; background falls off into soft blur.

Lighting & mood:
- Low-key, controlled directional light from the left (softbox feel), gentle specular highlights on metal edges, soft shadows.
- Cool/neutral color grading (deep navy, graphite, cool gray), restrained saturation, high-end dark-interface aesthetic.

Style:
- Photorealistic, modern, minimal, no clutter, no bright white surfaces, no warm sunlight.
- Clean materials: anodized aluminum, matte plastic, textured desk surface.

Camera details (if supported):
- 50mm lens, f/1.8, ISO 200, 1/125s, subtle film grain, slight vignette.

Constraints:
- No people, no hands, no visible brand marks, no neon colors.
- Background should be subdued and slightly darkened so it feels integrated with a dark product interface.

Optional variants:
- Swap laptop for a tablet with stylus.
- Replace checklist with a kanban-style column view labeled “Backlog / Doing / Done” using “{PROJECT}”.

title: Turn a messy topic into a focused research plan (questions, sources, search terms)
content:
Help me research the topic below and turn it into a clear, actionable plan I can follow.

Topic:
[Paste the topic / question / problem here]

My context (optional):
- What I already know:
- Why I’m researching this:
- Time budget:
- Audience (me/team/client):

What I want from you (keep it practical, not academic):
1) A one-paragraph framing of the problem: what it is, what it isn’t, and the most important assumptions.
2) 8–12 specific research questions, grouped into 3–5 themes.
3) A quick glossary: key terms I should understand (with plain-English definitions).
4) Search strategy:
   - 20 targeted search queries (mix of broad, narrow, and “comparison” queries)
   - 10 advanced query variations using operators (quotes, site:, filetype:, OR, minus)
5) Source map:
   - Where to look first (e.g., standards bodies, vendor docs, academic, reputable blogs, forums)
   - What each source type is good for + what it’s bad for
6) Credibility checklist: red flags vs green flags, and how to verify claims.
7) A lightweight note-taking template I can copy (sections + example bullets).
8) A 7-day or 2-week research schedule (depending on my time budget), with daily outputs.
9) “If you only read 5 things” shortlist: what those things should be (types of resources) and why.
10) A final deliverable outline (what my finished research should look like) with headings and bullet points.

Style requirements:
- Friendly, straightforward, and skimmable.
- Use bullets and short sections.
- If my topic is too vague, make reasonable assumptions and list them clearly before proceeding.

title: Turn a rough feature idea into a clear UX spec + acceptance criteria
content:
Take this rough feature idea and turn it into a compact UX spec a product team can implement.

Context
- Product: [what it is]
- Target user: [who]
- Core problem: [pain point]
- Feature idea (messy notes):
  [paste notes]
- Platform: [web / iOS / Android]
- Constraints: [time, tech, legal, accessibility, brand]

Output (use these headings)
1) One-sentence summary
2) User story (As a… I want… so that…)
3) Success criteria (3–6 measurable outcomes)
4) Primary user flow (step-by-step, max 10 steps)
5) Screens/states needed (list each state and what changes)
6) Key UI components (inputs, controls, system feedback)
7) Content requirements (exact microcopy for buttons, empty states, errors)
8) Edge cases to handle (at least 8; be specific)
9) Accessibility checklist (WCAG-minded, practical)
10) Analytics events (event name + properties)
11) Acceptance criteria (Given/When/Then bullets)

Rules
- Keep it structured and implementation-ready, not a narrative.
- Make assumptions only if necessary; list them under “Assumptions.”
- Avoid design trends and vague language—be concrete about behavior.

title: Turn a messy bug report into an actionable debugging plan (with hypotheses)
content:
I’m going to paste a bug report and whatever context I have. Turn it into an actionable debugging plan I can follow.

Input:
- Product/feature: [what area of the code]
- Stack/runtime: [language/framework/runtime]
- Environment: [local/staging/prod], [OS], [version(s)]
- Symptom: [what users/devs see]
- Frequency: [always/sometimes/%]
- Recent changes: [PRs, deploys, config flips]
- Logs/errors: [paste]
- Steps to reproduce (if any): [paste]
- Expected vs actual: [paste]
- Constraints: [can/can’t change, time pressure, risk]

Output requirements:
1) Restate the issue in one sentence so it’s unambiguous.
2) List 5–10 plausible root-cause hypotheses, ranked by likelihood and impact. Tie each hypothesis to specific clues from the input.
3) For the top 3 hypotheses, give:
   - the fastest verification test (exact commands, log filters, breakpoints, or code locations to inspect)
   - what result would confirm vs falsify it
   - the next step depending on the outcome
4) Identify missing information that would change the ranking (ask only the highest-leverage questions).
5) Provide a minimal instrumentation plan: what to log/trace/metric, where to add it, and how to avoid noise or sensitive data.
6) Suggest a safe mitigation I can ship quickly (feature flag, rollback, guard clause, retry/backoff, circuit breaker), plus risks.
7) If you propose code changes, include small, targeted snippets or pseudocode and mention what tests to add/adjust.

Style: modern and direct. Prefer checklists and concrete next actions over long explanations.

title: Turn messy dev notes into a prioritized task list for today
content:
I’m going to paste a block of messy development notes. Convert it into a practical, prioritized task list I can execute today.

Requirements:
- Output format:
  1) Top 3 outcomes for today (short, measurable)
  2) Task list (prioritized). Each task: [Impact: H/M/L] [Effort: S/M/L] [Owner: me] [Timebox: minutes] + 1-line acceptance criteria
  3) Quick decisions needed (if any) with the smallest set of options
  4) Risks/unknowns to unblock first (bullet list)
- Keep tasks concrete (verbs, specific files/components if mentioned).
- If something is vague, rewrite it as a “Define/Verify” task with clear next step.
- Don’t invent work that isn’t supported by the notes.

Here are the notes:

<PASTE NOTES HERE>

title: Research Paper Extractor: JSON parser + validation rules
content:
Extract structured data from the research paper text I paste below.

Output ONLY valid minified JSON that matches the schema. No commentary, no markdown.

Task
- Parse the paper into a machine-usable record for a research notes database.
- If a field is missing or not stated, use null (not an empty string) and add a short note in missing_info[].
- Do not guess numbers, dates, or claims.

Schema (strict)
{
  "paper": {
    "title": "string|null",
    "authors": ["string"],
    "year": "number|null",
    "venue": "string|null",
    "doi": "string|null",
    "url": "string|null"
  },
  "problem": {
    "one_sentence": "string|null",
    "motivation": "string|null"
  },
  "contributions": ["string"],
  "methods": {
    "approach": "string|null",
    "data": {
      "source": "string|null",
      "domain": "string|null",
      "sample_size": "number|null",
      "time_range": "string|null",
      "inclusion_criteria": "string|null",
      "exclusion_criteria": "string|null"
    },
    "features_or_inputs": ["string"],
    "model_or_algorithm": "string|null",
    "baselines": ["string"],
    "implementation_details": ["string"]
  },
  "experiments": {
    "setup": "string|null",
    "metrics": ["string"],
    "ablations": ["string"],
    "hardware": "string|null"
  },
  "results": {
    "key_findings": ["string"],
    "tables_or_figures": ["string"],
    "numbers": [
      {
        "metric": "string",
        "value": "string",
        "dataset_or_split": "string|null",
        "baseline": "string|null",
        "notes": "string|null"
      }
    ]
  },
  "limitations": ["string"],
  "threats_to_validity": ["string"],
  "ethics": {
    "privacy": "string|null",
    "bias_fairness": "string|null",
    "safety": "string|null"
  },
  "reproducibility": {
    "code_available": "boolean|null",
    "data_available": "boolean|null",
    "artifacts": ["string"],
    "reproduction_notes": ["string"]
  },
  "citations": {
    "referenced_works": ["string"],
    "related_work_clusters": ["string"]
  },
  "keywords": ["string"],
  "missing_info": ["string"],
  "confidence": {
    "overall": "number",
    "field_level": {
      "paper": "number",
      "problem": "number",
      "methods": "number",
      "results": "number"
    }
  }
}

Validation rules
- confidence.overall and confidence.field_level.* are numbers from 0 to 1.
- authors, contributions, and keywords must be arrays (use [] if none found).
- year must be a number (e.g., 2021), not a string.
- results.numbers.value must be a string exactly as written in the paper (e.g., "87.3%", "0.912", "±0.03").
- Do not merge distinct metrics; keep separate entries.
- If the paper includes multiple datasets/splits, include dataset_or_split.
- If code/data availability is unclear, set to null.

Input
Paste the full paper text (or abstract + methods + results) between the markers.

---BEGIN PAPER---

---END PAPER---
